explicit_text: I was born in Termini Imerese, Sicily, in 1888, and in 1910 I married Nicola Bondi and moved to Indianapolis. I was skilled with a needle and brought a trousseau filled with bedding and clothing as a symbol of my heritage and skills. Together with Nicola, we sold produce in Indianapolis and raised four children before I sadly passed away from tuberculosis in 1921 at the age of thirty-three. My legacy lives on through the items I left behind, now preserved and displayed to honor our Italian immigrant journey.
implicit_text: I was born in a small Sicilian town in 1888, and in 1910 I married a man named Nicola and moved to Indianapolis. I was skilled with a needle and brought a trousseau filled with bedding and clothing as a symbol of my heritage and skills. Together with Nicola, we sold produce in Indianapolis and raised four children before I sadly passed away from tuberculosis in 1921 at the age of thirty-three. My legacy lives on through the items I left behind, now preserved and displayed to honor our Italian immigrant journey.

explicit_text: Nicola Bondi was my husband, an Italian emigrant who lived in Indianapolis and returned to Sicily to find me as his wife in 1910. He was a produce vendor, selling fruits and vegetables from a horse-drawn cart in Indianapolis. Together, we built a life in the city, raising four children and working hard to support our family. Nicola's decision to bring me to America shaped the course of our lives and the future of our descendants in Indianapolis.

explicit_text: I was born in Termini Imerese, a town on the northern coast of Sicily, Italy, in 1888. It was an agricultural area that was depressed and heavily taxed around the turn of the century, which made life difficult for many of us. This environment shaped my early years before I left to join my husband in Indianapolis in 1910. Termini Imerese is where my roots and traditions began, and it remained a significant part of my identity throughout my life.

explicit_text: Italy was my homeland, specifically the southern region of Sicily where I was born. At the time, Italy was newly unified but faced political hardship and economic struggles, especially for farmers like my family. Many Italians, including myself, left between 1890 and 1920 seeking better opportunities abroad. Italy's challenges pushed me to start a new life in the United States with my husband Nicola.

explicit_text: Indianapolis became my new home after I married Nicola Bondi in 1910 and immigrated from Sicily. It was a city with a growing Italian immigrant community where many of us worked in produce markets, railroads, and factories. Nicola and I sold fruits and vegetables here, with me running a stand at the Market House and him selling from a horse-drawn cart. Our family grew in Indianapolis, and despite hardships, our descendants continue to live and thrive here today.

explicit_text: Palermo was the port city in Sicily from which Nicola and I sailed to America in 1910. We embarked on the S.S. Martha Washington for a difficult week-long voyage across the Atlantic. Leaving Palermo marked the beginning of my immigrant journey to the United States and a new life in Indianapolis. It was a bittersweet departure from my homeland but full of hope for the future.

explicit_text: The S.S. Martha Washington was the ship that carried me and Nicola from Palermo to Ellis Island in 1910. The voyage took about a week and was challenging, marking the start of my journey to America. Arriving on this ship was a pivotal moment, as it brought me from Sicily to a new life in Indianapolis. The experience was both daunting and hopeful as I left my old world behind.

explicit_text: Ellis Island was where Nicola and I arrived on May 17, 1910, after our transatlantic voyage. It was the gateway for millions of immigrants like me seeking a new life in the United States. Stepping onto Ellis Island was both overwhelming and exciting, as it marked the official start of our American journey. From there, we traveled to Indianapolis to begin building our family and livelihood.

Leaving Italy was difficult, but it was necessary to seek better opportunities in America. My heritage from Italy remained a strong part of my identity throughout my life.

Despite leaving, Sicily's culture and traditions stayed with me and influenced my family’s identity in America.

explicit_text: The United States was the land of opportunity that Nicola and I sought when we left Sicily in 1910. We settled in Indianapolis, joining thousands of other Italian immigrants striving to build new lives. America offered us a chance to work, raise a family, and preserve our culture while adapting to a new way of life. My journey here was challenging but ultimately hopeful for my descendants’ future.

explicit_text: World War I was a looming event during the years after Nicola and I settled in Indianapolis. It created pressure for Italian immigrants like us to assimilate into American culture as tensions rose. The war years influenced how our community balanced maintaining Italian traditions while adapting to life in the United States. It was a time of change that shaped the identity of the second generation born here.

explicit_text: In Indianapolis, I operated my own produce stand at the Market House, which was the predecessor to today's City Market. This was where I sold fresh fruits and vegetables, contributing to our family's livelihood alongside Nicola, who sold from a horse-drawn cart. The Market House was a central place for many Italian immigrants working in food-related businesses. It was an important part of our daily life and connection to the community.
